name: rbac
context: Control access to Kubernetes resources with RBAC — Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ServiceAccounts
info: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) governs who can do what in a Kubernetes cluster. Roles define permissions (verbs on resources), RoleBindings grant those permissions to users, groups, or ServiceAccounts. Namespace-scoped Roles and RoleBindings control access within a namespace. Cluster-scoped ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings control cluster-wide access. Agents use RBAC to restrict CI/CD pipelines to specific namespaces, grant read-only access for monitoring, limit Pod creation permissions, and secure ServiceAccounts for workloads. This skill covers Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, ServiceAccounts, and common RBAC patterns.
triggers: check who can access a resource
bind a role to a user
body:
# RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)

RBAC controls access to Kubernetes resources. The model: **Roles** define what actions are allowed, **Bindings** grant those roles to subjects (users, groups, ServiceAccounts).

## RBAC Resources

| Resource | Scope | Purpose |
|----------|-------|---------|
| `Role` | Namespace | Permissions within a single namespace |
| `ClusterRole` | Cluster | Permissions across all namespaces or on cluster-scoped resources |
| `RoleBinding` | Namespace | Grants a Role or ClusterRole within a namespace |
| `ClusterRoleBinding` | Cluster | Grants a ClusterRole across the entire cluster |
| `ServiceAccount` | Namespace | Identity for Pods and workloads |

## Role (namespace-scoped)

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
  namespace: production
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]           # core API group
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]  
    resources: ["services", "endpoints"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
```

## ClusterRole (cluster-scoped)

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
    resources: ["*"]
    verbs: ["*"]
  - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["ingresses"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
```

## RoleBinding

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: read-pods-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice@example.com
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: ci-deployer
    namespace: ci
  - kind: Group
    name: backend-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```

## ClusterRoleBinding

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: cluster-monitoring
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: prometheus
    namespace: monitoring
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view                  # built-in read-only ClusterRole
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```

## ServiceAccounts

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: ci-deployer
  namespace: ci
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/ci-deployer  # AWS IAM role
```

```bash
# Create a ServiceAccount
kubectl create serviceaccount ci-deployer -n ci

# Generate a token for the ServiceAccount
kubectl create token ci-deployer -n ci --duration=1h

# Use in Pod spec
```

```yaml
spec:
  serviceAccountName: ci-deployer
  containers:
    - name: deploy
      image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
```

## Built-in ClusterRoles

| ClusterRole | Permissions |
|-------------|------------|
| `cluster-admin` | Full access to everything (superuser) |
| `admin` | Full access within a namespace (when bound via RoleBinding) |
| `edit` | Read/write to most resources in a namespace (no RBAC, no quotas) |
| `view` | Read-only access to most resources in a namespace |

```bash
# Grant read-only access to a namespace
kubectl create rolebinding alice-view \
  --clusterrole=view \
  --user=alice@example.com \
  --namespace=production

# Grant full namespace admin
kubectl create rolebinding alice-admin \
  --clusterrole=admin \
  --user=alice@example.com \
  --namespace=staging
```

## RBAC Verbs

| Verb | HTTP Method | Description |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| `get` | GET | Read a single resource |
| `list` | GET | List resources |
| `watch` | GET (streaming) | Watch for changes |
| `create` | POST | Create a resource |
| `update` | PUT | Update a resource |
| `patch` | PATCH | Partially update a resource |
| `delete` | DELETE | Delete a resource |
| `deletecollection` | DELETE | Delete multiple resources |

## Checking Permissions

```bash
# Can I do X?
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n production
kubectl auth can-i delete pods --all-namespaces

# Can a ServiceAccount do X?
kubectl auth can-i get secrets -n production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:ci:ci-deployer

# Who can do X? (requires kubectl-who-can plugin)
kubectl who-can create pods -n production
```

## Common Patterns

### CI/CD Deployer
```yaml
# Role: can manage deployments and services in a namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deployer
  namespace: production
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["services", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
```

### Read-Only Monitoring
```yaml
# Bind the built-in 'view' ClusterRole
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: monitoring-view
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: monitoring
    namespace: monitoring
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace |

sections.title: Roles and ClusterRoles
sections.body: Roles define permissions as a list of rules. Each rule specifies apiGroups (e.g., '' for core, 'apps' for Deployments), resources (e.g., 'pods', 'deployments'), and verbs (e.g., 'get', 'list', 'create', 'delete'). Roles are namespace-scoped. ClusterRoles are cluster-scoped — they can also define permissions on cluster-scoped resources (nodes, namespaces, PVs). ClusterRoles can be bound per-namespace via RoleBinding or cluster-wide via ClusterRoleBinding.

sections.title: RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings
sections.body: Bindings connect Roles to subjects. RoleBinding grants a Role (or ClusterRole) within a single namespace. ClusterRoleBinding grants a ClusterRole across the entire cluster. Subjects: User (username from auth provider), Group (group from auth provider), ServiceAccount (namespace/name). A common pattern: define a ClusterRole once, bind it per-namespace with RoleBindings.

sections.title: ServiceAccounts
sections.body: ServiceAccounts provide identity for Pods. Every namespace has a 'default' ServiceAccount auto-mounted into Pods. Create custom ServiceAccounts for workloads that need specific permissions. Bind RBAC Roles to ServiceAccounts via RoleBindings. Generate tokens with `kubectl create token <sa>`. On cloud providers, ServiceAccounts can be linked to cloud IAM roles (AWS IRSA, GCP Workload Identity) for accessing cloud services without static credentials.

sections.title: Checking and debugging permissions
sections.body: Use `kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource>` to check your own permissions. Add `--as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<name>` to impersonate a ServiceAccount. Use `kubectl auth can-i --list` to see all allowed actions. Install kubectl-who-can plugin to find all subjects with a given permission. Debug 403 errors by checking: 1) correct ServiceAccount on Pod, 2) RoleBinding exists, 3) Role has the right verbs/resources/apiGroups.

links.context: RBAC Roles and RoleBindings are namespace-scoped
RBAC controls who can read Secrets
RBAC controls who can create/update Deployments
Helm releases may need RBAC for their ServiceAccounts

related.note: Namespaces and RBAC together provide multi-tenancy
RBAC controls Secret access — critical for security

scripts.name: Check Permissions
scripts.description: Check what a user or ServiceAccount can do in a namespace
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
SUBJECT=${1:-}
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}

if [ -z "$SUBJECT" ]; then
  echo "Checking YOUR permissions in namespace $NS:"
  kubectl auth can-i --list -n "$NS"
else
  echo "Checking permissions for $SUBJECT in namespace $NS:"
  kubectl auth can-i --list -n "$NS" --as="$SUBJECT"
fi

scripts.name: Create Deployer ServiceAccount
scripts.description: Create a ServiceAccount with deployment permissions for CI/CD
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
SA_NAME=${1:-ci-deployer}
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}

echo "Creating ServiceAccount $SA_NAME in namespace $NS"
kubectl create serviceaccount "$SA_NAME" -n "$NS" --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

echo "Creating deployer Role..."
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: ${SA_NAME}-role
  namespace: ${NS}
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["services", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF

echo "Creating RoleBinding..."
kubectl create rolebinding "${SA_NAME}-binding" \
  --role="${SA_NAME}-role" \
  --serviceaccount="${NS}:${SA_NAME}" \
  -n "$NS" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

echo ""
echo "ServiceAccount $SA_NAME configured with deployer permissions in $NS"
echo "Generate a token with: kubectl create token $SA_NAME -n $NS"

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: developers, DevOps engineers, SREs, AI agents
